interview_metadata.interviewee_title: Baptist Minister and Civil Rights Leader, Pastor Emeritus of Mount Zion Baptist Church (Seattle)
interview_metadata.interviewer_affiliation: Virginia Tech / Southern Oral History Program

main_summary.summary:
Reverend Dr. Samuel Berry McKinney recounts a life rooted in the Black Baptist tradition and the social-gospel movement, from his birth in Flint, Michigan, on December 28, 1926, to his decades-long pastorate at Mount Zion Baptist Church in Seattle. He describes his father, Wade Hampton McKinney, a Georgia sharecropper's son who entered Atlanta Baptist College (later Morehouse College) in 1910 at age 18 in the fifth grade and went on to Rochester Theological Seminary, and his mother, Annie Ruth Berry McKinney, who arrived at Spelman Seminary in 1916 and was among a small group of women who technically graduated from Morehouse before Spelman became a college in 1929. McKinney explains how the social-gospel tradition (rooted in Walter Rauschenbusch's teaching at Rochester and in Luke 4) shaped his father's ministry, first at Mount Olive Baptist Church in Flint and then for over thirty-four years at Antioch Baptist Church in Cleveland, where the family settled when McKinney was eighteen months old.

McKinney entered Morehouse in 1944 at age seventeen alongside a fifteen-year-old freshman named Martin Luther King Jr., who had used a Georgia wartime program allowing students to skip the twelfth grade. He recalls being drafted into the segregated U.S. Army Air Corps, training at Sheppard Field in Wichita Falls, Texas, and witnessing German prisoners of war served at white-tablecloth tables while Black American soldiers ate on cardboard plates in a colored dining room. After his discharge, McKinney completed Morehouse on the GI Bill under the presidency of Benjamin Elijah Mays (1941-1967), shifted from a pre-law track to the ministry under the influence of professor George Kelsey, and went on to Colgate Rochester Divinity School.

McKinney describes his decision to accept the call to Mount Zion Baptist Church in Seattle (which he received in late November 1957 and began the first Sunday of February 1958, after a prior pastorate in Providence, Rhode Island). He recounts inviting Martin Luther King Jr. to speak in Seattle in 1961 - King's last solo trip, per King himself - and the dispute with First Presbyterian Church, which agreed to host the larger event and then attempted to cancel; the event was relocated downhill to another venue. He recalls receiving an apology letter from First Presbyterian Church in 1998, thirty-seven or thirty-eight years later, and discusses parallels with the federal apology and reparations paid to Japanese Americans interned after Pearl Harbor. McKinney details his role in the Central Area Civil Rights Committee (organized 1963, with Rev. John Adams of First AME as a leading figure), boycotts and a successful march against Frederick & Nelson department store that opened employment, and his discomfort with the Black Panther chapter that formed in Seattle in the late 1960s. He recounts building Mount Zion's new sanctuary in 1974 (financed in part by the American Baptist Home Mission Society and a church credit union after local banks refused), establishing the Seattle Opportunities Industrialization Center (OIC) inspired by Rev. Leon H. Sullivan of Philadelphia, helping organize Liberty Bank, and serving on community development efforts including the housing project that became the Samuel Berry McKinney Manor. He closes with reflections on the George Washington Bush settler history of the Washington territory, the historical Oregon exclusion law (repealed 1926), and his hope for President Obama and for cross-racial coalition.
key_themes: Morehouse College and the Mays-King Lineage
Segregated Military Service in World War II
main_summary.historical_significance: McKinney's interview is an unusually rich firsthand account of how Morehouse College and the Black Baptist social-gospel tradition produced a national network of activist clergy who carried Movement organizing into Pacific Northwest cities rarely centered in Civil Rights historiography. His personal recollection of a fifteen-year-old Martin Luther King Jr. as a Morehouse freshman, his eyewitness account of the 1961 King visit to Seattle and the First Presbyterian Church controversy, and his documentation of Northwest segregation (including the Oregon exclusion law repealed only in 1926, the housing-discrimination calls he received as a new arrival, and the Frederick & Nelson employment campaign) fill important gaps in the geographic scope of the Movement record. His later institution-building work (the OIC, Liberty Bank, the McKinney Manor) documents the post-1965 transition from direct action to economic and community development that has been less extensively recorded than the Southern movement years.

errors: Summary states McKinney's father served Antioch Baptist Church 'over thirty-four years'; transcript says '34 and a half years until he passed in January, 1963.' Restatement is faithful but lightly rounded.

Summary describes Mount Zion's new sanctuary as built in 1974; transcript says the building was 'replaced in 1974' (segment 957). Wording is supported but could be interpreted as completion date rather than groundbreaking; left as transcript paraphrase.

Summary frames the First Presbyterian apology as arriving 'thirty-seven or thirty-eight years later'; transcript supports this range (segments 449-450 say '37 or 38 years ago, even justice was done to your church', and 471 says '38, 39 years later'). Range is faithful but inconsistent in transcript itself.

Summary calls Rev. John Adams 'of First AME'; transcript says 'pastor first in me at the time' (segment 825) which is a transcription artifact - the speaker likely said 'First AME' but the text as preserved is ambiguous. Marked partially supported in citation audit.
improvements: Quote McKinney's own words on the 1961 King visit and the First Presbyterian withdrawal directly, with timestamps, to anchor the narrative more securely.
Add the transcript's explicit reference to the 1962 Seattle World's Fair (segment 391) as context for why the larger venue was sought.
Surface McKinney's reflection on the 'Juneteenth factor' (segments 672-705) as a distinct theme - it is one of the most analytically distinctive parts of the interview.

chapters.title: Family Roots: Sharecropper Father, Morehouse Parents
chapters.summary: McKinney introduces himself as born December 28, 1926, in Flint, Michigan. His father, Wade Hampton McKinney (born outside Gainesville, Georgia, on July 19, 1892), was one of twelve children of a Georgia sharecropper. At age eighteen and in the fifth grade, McKinney's father was encouraged by his pastor at St. John Baptist Church in Gainesville to pursue further education and entered Atlanta Baptist College in 1910 (which became Morehouse College in 1914, named for Henry Lyman Morehouse of the Northern Baptist Convention). He received his high school diploma in 1916 and graduated from Morehouse in 1920, then attended Rochester Theological Seminary. McKinney details the founding of Spelman in the basement of Friendship Baptist Church in Atlanta by sisters Sophia B. Packard and Harriet E. Giles, the Rockefeller funding through Laura Spelman Rockefeller, the 1929 Atlanta University consolidation that left Morehouse, Spelman, and Atlanta University each retaining autonomy, and the Colgate-Rochester merger that same year. His mother, Annie Ruth Berry McKinney, came to Spelman from Birmingham in 1916 (one of six children, four of them girls who all attended Spelman). She finished in 1921 as one of thirteen or seventeen women who 'technically graduated from Morehouse,' since Spelman did not become a college until 1929.
related_events: Founding of Atlanta Baptist College (1867) / renaming to Morehouse (1914)
Booker T. Washington's 'Cast Down Your Bucket Where You Are'

improvements: Add the transcript's reference to McKinney's grandfather as a Baptist minister who acquired land per Washington's 'let down your bucket' philosophy.
Include the detail that every place King stopped on the Selma-to-Montgomery march was on land owned by Black people, which McKinney connects to this land-ownership tradition.

chapters.title: Father's Ministry in Flint and Cleveland; Social Gospel
chapters.summary: McKinney describes his father's first pastorate at Mount Olive Baptist Church in Flint, Michigan, beginning in 1923, his parents' marriage in 1924, the birth of McKinney's brother in 1925, and his own birth in 1926. In 1928, his father was called to Antioch Baptist Church, which he served 'thirty-four and a half years' until his death on January 18, 1963 (he retired at the end of 1962). McKinney explains how Morehouse-trained Black ministers were able to teach mainly at historically Black colleges and how this transplant of Northern-trained Black intellectuals carried the social-gospel emphasis into the South. He discusses Dr. John Hope, longtime Morehouse president (who 'looked like a white man'), and credits Walter Rauschenbusch (who taught at Rochester Theological Seminary and died while McKinney's father was still a student) as a key apostle of the social-justice gospel. McKinney summarizes the social gospel through Jesus's reading of Isaiah 61 in Luke 4 ('the Spirit of the Lord God is upon me because he has anointed me to preach the gospel, to free the captives, to set at liberty those who are bound, to declare the acceptable year of the Lord').
related_events: Walter Rauschenbusch and the Social Gospel Movement

improvements: Add McKinney's account of his father being one of John Hope's later students at Morehouse.

chapters.title: Morehouse, the Young Martin King, and Wartime Army Air Corps
chapters.summary: McKinney entered Morehouse in 1944 at age seventeen. He recalls that fellow freshman Martin Luther King Jr., from Atlanta, was fifteen years old at the time, having taken advantage of a Georgia wartime program that allowed students who passed a test in the eleventh grade to bypass the twelfth and enter college directly. King's class of more than four hundred freshmen had only fifty upperclassmen, so the freshmen decided there would be no hazing. McKinney remembers that King 'took part in every oratorical contest and never won' at Morehouse, then went on to Crozer Theological Seminary outside Philadelphia and to Boston University. McKinney himself was drafted into the segregated Army Air Corps. After basic training at Sheppard Field in Wichita Falls, Texas, his train stopped at a West Texas depot where his unit ate from cardboard plates with wooden forks in the colored dining room while German prisoners of war ate at white-tablecloth tables in the white dining room - 'These were supposed to be enemies of our country, because they were white.' He was stationed at Yuma, Arizona, then Biggs Field, California, and discovered Juneteenth for the first time when his Texas training base gave the day off. The GI Bill allowed both McKinney and his brother to finish college.
related_events: Segregation of the U.S. Armed Forces
King's Morehouse early-entry under wartime program

errors: Summary calls Sheppard Field a basic-training site in Wichita Falls, Texas; transcript supports this directly (segment 217).

Summary names 'Biggs Field, California'; transcript says 'biggest field, California' (segment 249) and again 'biggest field of California' (segment 266) - this is a Whisper-transcription artifact; the actual base is Biggs Army Airfield in El Paso, Texas. The interview text is ambiguous; preserved as the closest plausible mapping (Biggs) but flagged.
improvements: Clarify the Biggs Field transcription ambiguity in citation audit.
Include McKinney's near-AWOL impulse at the train stop as a vivid moment.

chapters.title: Benjamin Mays, Calling to the Ministry, and Role Models
chapters.summary: McKinney describes how Benjamin Elijah Mays - from South Carolina, educated at Bates College, Union Theological Seminary, and the University of Chicago (Ph.D.) - became president of Morehouse in 1940 and shaped the formation of an entire generation including King. McKinney recalls Mays's practical advice for navigating wartime Atlanta: if students had trouble with a white bus driver or streetcar operator, make a mental note of their name and badge number and report it to him rather than argue on the spot. McKinney shifted from a pre-law track to the ministry under the influence of George Kelsey, who taught the required Bible course and argued that 'the law can only function after something has been done' while religion functions on the front end. McKinney also describes the role models from his Cleveland boyhood: his father; Mordecai Wyatt Johnson, the Morehouse-graduate Baptist minister who was president of Howard University; A. Philip Randolph, Walter White of the NAACP, all of whom his father hosted at Antioch Baptist Church. He recounts the cultural impact of Joe Louis's victories (especially the knockout of Max Schmeling, which the Jewish community celebrated as Schmeling was 'one of Hitler's boys'), Jackie Robinson's integration of Major League Baseball, Larry Doby's integration of the American League with Cleveland, and the presence of Jesse Owens's family living five or six doors north of McKinney.
related_events: Joe Louis vs. Max Schmeling rematch (June 22, 1938)
Jackie Robinson's integration of the National League (1947)
Larry Doby's integration of the American League (1947)
Jesse Owens at the 1936 Berlin Olympics

errors: Summary states Mays became Morehouse president in 1940; transcript says '1941 to 1967' (segment 307). Discrepancy preserved per transcript; the widely documented date is 1940. Marked partially supported.

Summary names Mays's degree institutions as Bates College, Union Theological Seminary, and the University of Chicago; transcript reads '(Bates?) Virginia (?), Bates College, then Union Theological Seminary, and then earned the PhD from University of Chicago' (segment 138), which is a transcription stutter - the substance is supported but the wording is broken.
improvements: Quote McKinney's exact paraphrase of Kelsey's pothole-vs-ambulance analogy.
Note the Jewish-community context for the Schmeling celebration more prominently.

chapters.title: Inviting Dr. King to Seattle and the First Presbyterian Withdrawal
chapters.summary: McKinney recounts inviting Martin Luther King Jr. to Seattle in 1961 to speak for the brotherhood of Mount Zion Baptist Church, and notes that King told him this was the last trip he ever made by himself - having had too many near-misses with would-be assassins. With the 1962 Seattle World's Fair construction making Mount Zion's facilities too small, McKinney accepted the Seattle Presbytery executive's suggestion to approach First Presbyterian Church, which had a sanctuary that seated more than three thousand. McKinney describes what he understood as 'a solid, an antecedent agreement,' but as the date approached he received a letter from the church's ruling body stating the event could not be held there because Mount Zion 'had not followed the proper protocol.' At the subsequent meeting, the head of the church council (described by McKinney as roughly six-foot-two-and-a-half, with flowing white hair, a lawyer 'who could strike fear in the judge and the jury') confirmed the withdrawal and offered to pay Mount Zion's advertising costs. McKinney refused: 'We don't want your money... We'll tell the truth.' The group walked down the hill and immediately put a deposit on another venue, where the event was successfully held. McKinney recounts that in 1998, just before his retirement, he received an unsolicited letter from First Presbyterian Church apologizing for 'an injustice... 37 or 38 years ago,' and he met with the minister to discuss it; the minister attended McKinney's retirement.
related_events: First Presbyterian Church withdrawal and 1998 apology
Japanese American internment and federal reparations

errors: Summary says 'in 1998'; transcript says 'Just before I retired in 1998' (segment 441), which is supported.
improvements: Reference McKinney's connection between the apology and the federal apology / reparations to interned Japanese Americans.

chapters.title: Northwest Black History: George Washington Bush and the Oregon Exclusion Law
chapters.summary: McKinney provides a historical reframing of the Pacific Northwest's racial geography. He notes that the first white settlers came from the Middle South - Tennessee, Kentucky, parts of Virginia - and that the Oregon Territory had a law (not repealed until 1926) that made it illegal for Black people to permanently settle in the territory. He observes that he had not known of the law until he moved out West. He recounts the story of George Washington Bush - whom he is careful to distinguish from the presidential Bush family - a Black man who led a wagon train west from Missouri; when they were told at Portland they could not stay, they crossed the Columbia to Fort Vancouver and were directed to homestead land around Puget Sound (near present-day Lacey/Olympia, Washington). McKinney notes that one of George Washington Bush's sons sat in the last territorial legislature and the first state legislature of Washington, and tells a hecker that without Bush's wagon train Washington could have become part of British Columbia. He frames the 'Juneteenth factor' as a partial explanation for why people who migrated to the Pacific Northwest from west of the Mississippi (Louisiana, Texas, Arkansas, Oklahoma) had been freed two years after the rest, with consequent generational lag. He describes coal mining at Coal Creek (near Mercer Island), the mass grave of young miners from Wales and Scotland, and the Roslyn, Washington, mines where Black miners were brought in as scab labor before some migrated to Kennydale.
related_events: George Washington Bush's wagon train migration
Roslyn coal mining and Black scab labor recruitment

errors: Summary refers to Henry Lewis and Clark as Lewis and Clark; transcript references only 'Clark, those two fellas' as a fragment (segments 556-558). Supported partially.
Summary states McKinney came to Seattle from a Providence, Rhode Island pastorate; this is supported (segment 651) but the duration was 'three years and three months,' not summarized in this chapter.
improvements: Add McKinney's explicit framing of the 'Juneteenth factor' as his own analytical contribution (one of the most distinctive parts of the interview).
Note that McKinney explicitly says George Washington Bush is unrelated to the presidential Bushes.

chapters.title: Arrival in Seattle, Housing Discrimination, and Frederick & Nelson March
chapters.summary: McKinney received the call to Mount Zion Baptist Church in late November 1957 and began the first Sunday of February 1958, having previously pastored in Providence, Rhode Island, for three years and three months. He recounts difficulty finding housing in Seattle: when he called about advertised rentals, callers would ask 'are you colored?' before refusing to rent. His wife, who 'was a purist when it came to language,' would also call - and when they showed up in person to view a place, 'there was a different story.' On the surface, Seattle seemed better than what people had migrated from, but McKinney describes a structural racism articulated in the saying that the South 'didn't care how close blacks and whites got, so long as we didn't get too high' while the North 'didn't care how high we got, provided we didn't get too close.' McKinney describes the march on Frederick & Nelson, Seattle's then-major downtown department store (now a Nordstrom's), as the pivotal action that opened Black employment in such retail spaces. He recalls a critic in his own congregation who opposed the march and then applied for a job after it succeeded.

improvements: Date the Frederick & Nelson march if McKinney provides a year (he does not explicitly in this transcript).

Name McKinney's wife (Louise McKinney) if the transcript supports - here the transcript says 'It belonged to my wife, Carl' (segment 786), which appears to be a Whisper error; left unnamed in summary.

chapters.title: The Central Area Civil Rights Committee and the Black Panthers
chapters.summary: McKinney describes the Central Area Civil Rights Committee, the umbrella organization formed in 1963 that drew from the NAACP, the Urban League, and other Seattle Black groups to coordinate work in the Central Area. He names Rev. John Adams, then pastor of First AME Church Seattle (now a retired AME bishop living in Atlanta), as one of the most successful leaders of the group. McKinney recounts the school strike (a stay-at-home from de facto segregated schools) and other actions. He acknowledges later tensions with more radical groups including the Seattle Black Panther chapter that formed in the late 1960s: 'Not too well' was how the established ministers and the Panthers worked together. McKinney attributes this in part to generational difference and to some Panthers seeing 'the ministers as the enemy more than discrimination,' and to outside parties trying to play one group against the other. He notes that some Panthers later matured.

errors: Summary identifies Rev. John Adams as 'pastor of First AME Church Seattle'; transcript reads 'pastor first in me at the time' (segment 825), a transcription artifact. The 'First AME' identification is inferential. Marked partially supported.
improvements: Add the transcript's North-South comparison aphorism in McKinney's own words (it is one of his most pointed statements).
Note that the state-level antidiscrimination body McKinney references by partial name is the Washington State Board Against Discrimination.

chapters.title: Building Institutions: Sanctuary, Credit Union, OIC, Liberty Bank, McKinney Manor
chapters.summary: McKinney explains that one of the things that drew him to Mount Zion was the church's desire to build. The congregation bought up surrounding land, built a new sanctuary 'replaced in 1974,' and established a credit union so members could 'take care of their own needs' - which was also the financing source for the new sanctuary, alongside support from the American Baptist Home Mission Society, after 'we didn't get money from one single local bank.' McKinney describes participating in launching the Seattle Opportunities Industrialization Center (Seattle OIC) at 22nd and Jackson, modeled on Rev. Leon H. Sullivan's Philadelphia OIC movement. When President Reagan 'pulled the rug out' of community funding, Representative Jim McDermott helped have the state take over the facility, which became the Seattle Vocational Institute under the community-college system. McKinney also describes helping organize Liberty Bank (which 'later went out of business') at 24th and Union. He references the 'McKinney Manor' housing complex built through government programs, and the broader work of community development including the Camp / Setahoe program. He frames all of this work as the constructive complement to direct-action protest.
related_events: Mount Zion Baptist Church sanctuary build (1974)
Reagan-era cuts to community development funding

improvements: Specify the address of Liberty Bank's main building (24th and Union, per segment 932).
Note that McKinney is uncertain about the fate of his safety deposit box at Liberty Bank when the facility closed.

chapters.title: Demographic Change and Reflections on Obama, Coalitions, and Progress
chapters.summary: McKinney describes the demographic transformation of Seattle's Central Area, where the largest new Black population is now from various countries on the African continent (he notes Ethiopian and Haitian church communities). He recounts being told by a meeting interlocutor that African Americans are now 'the number three non-white group in the community' and responding by complicating that statistic: Hispanic identity is 'not ethnicity but tone,' so there are 'a lot of Black Hispanics,' and Africa is 'not a country, it's a continent' with as many internal differences as among 'white people.' He recalls advising community members not to sell Central Area property and many regretting that they did. He closes the interview with reflections on the Obama presidency, expressing frustration that Obama 'has bent over too much' trying to placate people 'who have no intention of working with him because they would rather see the country suffer than for him to claim any credit.' He envisions the next phase as a Hillary Clinton presidency, expresses hope that the 'nuts and the fools' who fetishize guns will come to their senses, and prays that 'sense which is not common to everybody will prevail.' The interviewer thanks him and the material is committed to the Library of Congress.
related_events: Demographic change in Seattle Central Area

improvements: Add McKinney's reflection on the loss of 'neighborhood' as the foundation for community.

interview_metadata.interviewee_title: Baptist Minister and Civil Rights Leader, Pastor Emeritus of Mount Zion Baptist Church (Seattle)
interview_metadata.interviewer_affiliation: Virginia Tech / Southern Oral History Program

evidence: Opens with birth date and family lineage: 'I was born December 28, 1926 in Flint, Michigan' (Segment 9, 00:01:14,000)

Threads narrative chronologically from grandfather's sharecropping through Morehouse, military, ministry, Seattle: 'I came here and I received the call... the latter part of November 1957... I moved in and began the first Sunday in February 1958' (Segment 644-645, 01:07:17,000)
story_structure_coherence.justification: The interview follows a broadly chronological arc - childhood, family roots, Morehouse, the military, ministry, Seattle - with occasional thematic detours that McKinney signals ('let me back up just a little bit so you can get a flavor'). Transitions are mostly explicit, though some sections (e.g., the Pacific Northwest history detour) interrupt the chronology for extended periods. The interview closes with reflections on progress, giving it a satisfying frame.

most_vivid_moment.quote: We had cardboard plates wooden forks and somebody said, hey, come here and look. We looked from where we were in the colored dining room, in the white dining room. There were tablecloths, then in the silverware and German prisoners of war. Sitting down to the table eating, we had on the United States Army uniform. And these were supposed to be enemies of our country, because they were white.

evidence: Specific founding details: 'Atlanta Baptist College in 1910, which became Morehouse College in 1914' (Segment 15, 00:02:45,000)
Specific dates for father's career: 'served that church well... thirty-four and a half years until he passed in January, 1963' (Segment 101, 00:15:11,000)
Vivid scene of First Presbyterian meeting with the 'six-two-and-a-half, flowing white hair' lawyer (Segment 406-417, 00:49:46,000)
vivid_details_examples.justification: McKinney provides exceptional precision across family genealogy, institutional history, and Seattle civic events. Names, dates, addresses (22nd and Jackson, 24th and Union, 19th and Madison) are abundant. Some scenes are reconstructed with sensory detail (the German POW dining room, the lawyer at First Presbyterian); others are summarized at a higher level.

most_authentic_moment.quote: And no respect for us, and I asked somebody, I was up-set with what I saw. I asked somebody, how far was it from where we were to Cleveland? They said about 1800 miles. And they asked me, are you thinking about going AWOL? I said, I'm thinking about going AWOL.

vulnerability_elements: Mentions his wife's passing 'in August' (Segment 1027) without elaboration
Admits 'I did not have, at that time, a commitment to nonviolence' (Segment 170, 00:23:18,000) - vulnerable admission about why he did not take the Montgomery pulpit before King
Acknowledges fellow congregation members criticized him for marching on Frederick & Nelson
emotional_authenticity.justification: McKinney shows real anger about the segregated military, dry humor about his hometown bus drivers, and quiet grief about his late wife. He admits the limit of his own pre-King nonviolence commitment, a candid moment. The register stays mostly composed and analytical rather than fully unguarded, but several emotional peaks register clearly.

narrative_quality.overall_assessment: McKinney is a polished and practiced narrator who deploys vivid set-pieces (the German POW dining room, the First Presbyterian confrontation, the heckler in the Washington audience) within an otherwise analytical and chronologically organized account. The interview has a clear structure, strong specificity, and moments of genuine emotional honesty - including a candid admission that he was not yet committed to nonviolence when he could have taken the Montgomery pulpit before King.

major_events: Morehouse College 1944-1948 as freshman classmate of Martin Luther King Jr. (Segments 140-147, 00:20:13,000-00:21:00,000)
Segregated Army Air Corps World War II service (Segments 207-265, 00:27:27,000-00:34:46,000)
Hosting King's 1961 Seattle visit and the First Presbyterian withdrawal (Segments 380-440, 00:46:59,000-00:55:00,000)
Co-founder of Central Area Civil Rights Committee 1963 (Segments 814-832, 01:20:43,000-01:23:50,000)
Co-organizer of Liberty Bank and Seattle OIC (Segments 913-936, 01:30:23,000-01:32:51,000)

key_figures.name: Martin Luther King Jr.
key_figures.relationship: Morehouse classmate (1944-48); host of King's last solo travel (1961)

key_figures.name: Adam Clayton Powell Jr.
key_figures.relationship: Annual church anniversary speaker at Antioch Baptist Church (1936-1946) hosted by McKinney's father

key_figures.name: Mordecai Wyatt Johnson
key_figures.relationship: Family friend and mentor figure to McKinney's father

key_figures.name: Rev. John Adams
key_figures.relationship: Co-leader of Seattle Central Area Civil Rights Committee

key_figures.name: Rev. Leon H. Sullivan
key_figures.relationship: OIC movement founder whose Philadelphia model McKinney brought to Seattle

unique_firsthand_perspective.unique_access: First-person memory of 15-year-old MLK Jr. as a Morehouse freshman; documented account of King's last solo trip (Seattle, 1961); insider account of Pacific Northwest civil rights organizing largely missing from Southern-centric Movement historiography.
unique_firsthand_perspective.justification: McKinney has a rare combination of national-figure proximity (Morehouse with King, Mays as mentor) and regional originality (decades of Seattle institution-building). His account of King's 1961 Seattle visit, the First Presbyterian withdrawal, and its 1998 apology is a primary-source moment unlikely to be duplicated elsewhere.

precision_examples: Mount Zion call date: 'latter part of November 1957... first Sunday in February 1958' (Segment 644-645, 01:07:17,000)
verifiable_claims: Annie Ruth Berry McKinney entered Spelman Seminary in 1916
Mount Zion Baptist Church Seattle was organized in 1890
Oregon Territory exclusion law repealed in 1926
Wade Hampton McKinney served Antioch Baptist Church for 34.5 years
Mount Zion's current sanctuary was built/replaced in 1974
specificity_of_details.justification: McKinney provides exceptional date and place precision throughout, including specific street intersections, founding years for multiple institutions, and personal milestone dates. Some narratives include estimated ranges ('37 or 38 years ago') but the precision overall is rare.

new_information: Pacific Northwest civil rights organizing infrastructure (Mount Zion, CACRC, Seattle OIC, Liberty Bank) that is underrepresented in Southern-centered Movement scholarship
Detailed first-person account of 15-year-old MLK as Morehouse freshman
Documentation of the First Presbyterian Church withdrawal of the 1961 King venue and its 1998 apology
The Oregon exclusion law and George Washington Bush wagon train as a Black settler-history thread for Washington state
Reagan-era cuts and the McDermott-led state rescue of the Seattle OIC infrastructure
understudied_aspects: Black Pacific Northwest civil rights organizing
Post-1965 Black institution-building (community-development corporations, Black banks, OIC infrastructure)
Northern (and Western) anti-Black housing and employment discrimination contemporaneous with the Southern Movement
fills_historical_gaps.narrative_complications: McKinney's account complicates a Southern-only frame for the Civil Rights Movement, foregrounds the Pacific Northwest as a site of structural racism (housing redlining, retail employment discrimination, even the Oregon exclusion law), and documents the post-direct-action shift to economic and community development. His skeptical assessment of the Seattle Black Panthers complicates radical narratives of the late 1960s.
fills_historical_gaps.historiographical_value: Historians of Pacific Northwest Black history, the Morehouse-Mays cohort, Northern civil rights organizing, and post-1965 Black economic development will find this interview substantive. The First Presbyterian Church incident and the 1998 apology is potentially a small-scale case study for the reparations and apologies literature.
fills_historical_gaps.justification: McKinney's interview substantially fills the West Coast gap in the Civil Rights History Project corpus and documents institution-building work that is harder to find than direct-action accounts. The 15-year-old King memory is a contribution to the King biographical record.

historical_value.overall_assessment: This interview's historical value is exceptional in two distinct senses: it gives a first-hand account of a major national figure (King at Morehouse), and it documents the structural racism and counter-organizing of the Pacific Northwest in a corpus that otherwise skews heavily Southern. McKinney's institution-building work (Mount Zion sanctuary, Seattle OIC, Liberty Bank, McKinney Manor) is the post-direct-action chapter of the Movement that is often underrepresented.

transformation_growth.transformation_arc: From pre-law student to social-gospel minister; from young Army draftee whose first reaction to segregation was contemplating going AWOL to a senior pastor and movement leader; from outsider in Seattle facing housing discrimination to dean of Black Seattle civic infrastructure.
key_turning_points: George Kelsey's Bible class at Morehouse: 'Sometimes people say you're preaching to me. I didn't know but he was speaking to me' (Segment 283-284, 00:36:56,000)
German POW dining-room moment at the West Texas depot (Segment 222-232, 00:29:49,000)
transformation_growth.before_after_contrast: McKinney moved from preparing for a legal career to seeing ministry as 'the front end' of justice work; from a single young Black serviceman experiencing institutional racism to a community-institution-builder; from new Seattle pastor refused housing to long-serving senior pastor whose name is on a housing development.
transformation_growth.inspirational_quality: Demonstrates the persistence of patient, building-oriented Black leadership across decades. The First Presbyterian apology arriving thirty-seven years late is a particular emblem of long-arc justice work.
transformation_growth.justification: McKinney articulates a clear vocational pivot and a regional career trajectory. He does not dwell on internal transformation extensively, however - his register is more analytic than confessional - so some of the change is implicit rather than explicitly narrated.

peak_moments.description: First Presbyterian Church withdrawal of the King 1961 venue and McKinney's confrontation with the church council leader
peak_moments.stakes: Whether King's first Seattle address would happen at all on its scheduled date, and whether Mount Zion would accept a paternalistic financial settlement in exchange for cancellation

peak_moments.description: Frederick & Nelson march and successful opening of Black employment
peak_moments.stakes: Whether organized direct action could break the de facto color line in major Seattle retail employment

peak_moments.description: Witnessing German POWs served at white-tablecloth tables while in Army uniform
peak_moments.stakes: The moral indignation of being treated worse than enemies-of-state by one's own country

conflict_types: institutional racism vs. organized community response
intergenerational tension within the Movement (Panthers vs. established ministers)
civilian Army of the U.S. vs. regular Army pay-discrimination structure
tension_conflict_triumph.emotional_range: From quiet indignation through pointed humor to triumph over discrimination
tension_conflict_triumph.justification: There are clear conflict set-pieces with real stakes, but the narrative tone is mostly retrospective and analytic rather than dramatic. McKinney describes confrontations from a settled remove, which limits the dramatic intensity of the telling.

universal_themes: building community in a new city
relatable_struggles: Calling about apartments and being refused on the basis of race
Feeling out of place in a segregated military
Watching one's own congregation member criticize one's activism and then benefit from it
human_moments: Mother's family disapproved of boxing but listened to Joe Louis fights anyway (Segments 312-336, 00:40:13,000-00:42:53,000)
Mother ignored son's question about her inherited land (Segment 60-61, 00:10:35,000)
His wife's home renovations being unrecognizable to the previous owners (Segments 1033-1039, 01:42:12,000)
relatable_experiences.connection_points: Family ambition, vocational uncertainty, the experience of being judged by strangers, loss of a spouse, and dissatisfaction with a national political climate are all broadly relatable.
relatable_experiences.justification: McKinney's framing is more institutional than personal, but enough family moments and small human episodes punctuate the narrative to make the human substrate clear. The Joe Louis-listening-on-the-porch scene and the Black-Puritan family ethic give the interview a domestic anchor.

emotional_resonance.overall_assessment: The interview's emotional resonance is grounded but reserved. McKinney is a senior figure looking back from a settled position; he prefers analysis to disclosure. The dramatic peaks are clear (the POW dining room, First Presbyterian, Frederick & Nelson) but they are recounted with composure rather than re-enactment. The universal themes - family legacy, vocational discernment, building - carry the resonance.

barriers: Some Baptist denominational references (American Baptist Convention, Northern Baptist Convention, Crozer, Colgate Rochester) assumed
Specific Seattle place-name references (Madrona, Kennydale, Coal Creek Parkway, Mercer Island) may require local context for non-Seattle audiences
language_clarity.justification: McKinney's diction is articulate, classroom-clear, and pastoral. He self-contextualizes major references (e.g., explaining the social gospel through Luke 4). Local Seattle references occasionally assume regional knowledge but are not insurmountable.

high_energy_sections: Texas train-stop encounter with German POWs (Segments 217-232, 00:29:05,000-00:30:48,000)
Heckling at a Washington state speaking engagement (Segments 577-638, 01:02:15,000-01:06:36,000)
slow_sections: Extended Pacific Northwest historical detour around Louisiana Purchase, Lewis and Clark, and Pacific Northwest settlement (Segments 539-630, 00:59:04,000-01:05:46,000) - dense and digressive
Atlanta University consolidation explanation (Segments 22-52, 00:04:24,000-00:09:40,000) - inside-baseball for non-HBCU audiences
narrative_flow.attention_sustainability: An attentive listener can sustain the full 1:49:13 but the Pacific-Northwest historical detour and the early Atlanta institutional history would benefit from chapter cuts in an edited version.
narrative_flow.justification: The narrative is well-paced in stretches and meanders in others. The interviewer (David Cline) facilitates without overinterrupting but occasionally must redirect after a long digression. Some transcription gaps surface as conversational sputter.

themes_connected: social gospel as the theological foundation of the Movement
Morehouse-Mays-King intellectual lineage as the cradle of Movement leadership
geographic scope of American racism beyond the South
long-arc institutional change requiring church-funded credit unions and Black-owned banks
broader_themes_connection.contemporary_relevance: McKinney explicitly compares the 1998 First Presbyterian apology to federal apologies and reparations to interned Japanese Americans, and connects 21st-century racial coalition-building to the Obama and Hillary Clinton political moment.
broader_themes_connection.meaning_making: McKinney consistently connects personal episodes to broader Movement and historical themes, making the 'so what?' explicit throughout.
broader_themes_connection.justification: McKinney is intentional about historical context - sometimes to the point of detour - and routinely points out that personal stories illuminate national patterns (segregation in the military, housing discrimination, the demographic transformation of the Central Area).

accessibility.overall_assessment: The interview is highly accessible from a language-clarity standpoint, somewhat uneven in pacing due to extended historical digressions, and strongly connected to broader themes. A general audience can follow McKinney's account without specialized background; targeted edits could improve flow for shorter-format reuse.

overall_assessment: Reverend Dr. Samuel Berry McKinney's interview is a high-value, late-life retrospective from a senior Black Baptist clergyman who connects the Morehouse-Mays-King intellectual lineage to a half-century of civil rights and community-development work in the Pacific Northwest. Its specificity (dates, addresses, institutions, family genealogy) is exceptional; its emotional register is composed and analytical rather than confessional. The interview fills a substantial geographic gap in the Civil Rights History Project corpus, documents King's last solo trip and the First Presbyterian Church incident, and chronicles the post-1965 institution-building chapter of the Movement (Mount Zion sanctuary, Seattle OIC, Liberty Bank, McKinney Manor). The narrative could benefit from sharper editing for pace - extended detours into Pacific Northwest settler history and Atlanta University consolidation slow the central arc - but the value of the material is high.
key_strengths: Exceptional first-person testimony bridging the Morehouse-Mays-King lineage (1944-48 Morehouse) and the Pacific Northwest civil rights infrastructure (1958-1998 Mount Zion)
Rich specificity on family genealogy, institutional history, dates, and Seattle addresses that historians can verify and reuse
Documents the First Presbyterian Church 1961 withdrawal and 1998 apology - a small but instructive case for the reparations/apologies literature
key_limitations: Composed analytical register limits the emotional immediacy of the most dramatic moments; the listener is often told about indignation rather than re-experiencing it
Extended historical digressions (Atlanta University consolidation, Pacific Northwest settler history, Louisiana Purchase) slow the central arc and assume some background knowledge

claims.claim: Samuel Berry McKinney was born December 28, 1926, in Flint, Michigan.
claims.supporting_excerpt: My name is Samuel Berry McKinney, as was indicated. I was born December 28, 1926 in Flint, Michigan. (Segment 9)
claims.rationale: Date and place stated directly by McKinney.

claims.claim: His father, Wade Hampton McKinney, was a Georgia sharecropper's son who entered Atlanta Baptist College in 1910 at age 18 in the fifth grade.
claims.supporting_excerpt: My father was a Baptist minister who was born in, outside of Gainesville, Georgia on July 19, 1892. And he was one of 12 children. His father, Wade Hampton McKinney... was a Georgia sharecropper. When my father was 18 and in the fifth grade, he was encouraged by his pastor of the St. John Baptist Church in Gainesville, Georgia... So he went to the Atlanta Baptist College in 1910, which became Morehouse College in 1914. (Segments 10-15)
claims.rationale: All elements (father's name shared with grandfather; sharecropper status; 1910 entry to Atlanta Baptist College at age 18 in the fifth grade) directly attested.

claims.claim: Atlanta Baptist College became Morehouse College in 1914, named for Henry Lyman Morehouse.
claims.supporting_excerpt: And in 1914 it became Morehouse College, named for Henry Lyman Morehouse, who had been the executive of the... It was then the Northern Baptist Convention. (Segment 26-27)

claims.claim: His father attended Rochester Theological Seminary in Rochester, New York.
claims.supporting_excerpt: He was accepted at the Rochester Theological Seminary in Rochester, New York, where he continued his education. (Segment 19)

claims.claim: His mother, Annie Ruth Berry McKinney, came to Spelman Seminary in 1916 from Birmingham, Alabama.
claims.supporting_excerpt: I'm saying all that to say that in 1916 a young lady from Birmingham, Alabama named Annie Ruth Berry McKinney McKinney, my mother, came to Spelman. (Segment 43)

claims.claim: His mother was one of thirteen or seventeen women who 'technically graduated from Morehouse' before Spelman became a college in 1929.
claims.supporting_excerpt: She became one of 13 or 17 women who technically graduated from Morehouse, which was a college. Spelman didn't become a college until 1929. (Segments 50-51)

claims.claim: Spelman was founded in the basement of Friendship Baptist Church in Atlanta by Sophia B. Packard and Harriet E. Giles.
claims.supporting_excerpt: But they organized a college for women in the basement of the Friendship Baptist Church in Atlanta. And it became Spelman College... These two sisters, Packard and Giles, I can't remember their first names. (Segments 30-33)
claims.rationale: Founders identified by surname; basement-of-Friendship-Baptist origin stated.

claims.claim: His father's first pastorate was at Mount Olive Baptist Church in Flint, Michigan, beginning in 1923.
claims.supporting_excerpt: My father's first church was in Flint, Michigan, Mount Olive Baptist Church. He went there in 1923. (Segments 95-96)

claims.claim: In 1928, his father was called to Antioch Baptist Church, which he served thirty-four and a half years until his death on January 18, 1963.
claims.supporting_excerpt: In 1928, my father was called to the Antioch Baptist Church. A church he served for 34 and a half years until he passed in January, 1963. Although he was the retirement, the end of 1962, he passed 18th of January, 1963. (Segments 100-102)

claims.claim: Walter Rauschenbusch taught at Rochester Theological Seminary and was a leading apostle of the social gospel.
claims.supporting_excerpt: During that period when my father was in college, there was a fellow who taught a roucher, a roucher theological seminary for a while, a Walter Rauschenbusch... He was declared one of the apostles of the social justice, gospel. (Segments 130-133)
claims.rationale: Directly stated; 'roucher' is a transcription artifact for 'Rochester'.

claims.claim: McKinney entered Morehouse in 1944 at age seventeen.
claims.supporting_excerpt: I was entered in 1944. And at age 17. (Segments 140-141)

claims.claim: Martin Luther King Jr. was a fellow freshman at Morehouse, age fifteen, having used a Georgia program to bypass the twelfth grade.
claims.supporting_excerpt: One of the classmates, one of the fellow freshmen, was a fellow from Atlanta... Squirt, named Martin Luther King Jr. was in the same freshman class. He was 15... if you took a test in the 11th grade and passed it, you could go automatically, you could bypass the 12th grade and go directly into college. And that's what Dr. King did. (Segments 143-156)

claims.claim: King 'took part in every oratorical contest and never won' at Morehouse.
claims.supporting_excerpt: It's interesting to note that to my knowledge, he took part in every oratorical contest and never won. That's kind of hard to believe, but it happened. (Segments 164-165)

claims.claim: King attended Crozer Theological Seminary outside Philadelphia and then Boston University.
claims.supporting_excerpt: He then went to Crozer Theological Seminary right outside of Philadelphia. And from that, Boston University. (Segments 166-167)

claims.claim: McKinney was drafted into the segregated U.S. Army Air Corps and trained at Sheppard Field in Wichita Falls, Texas.
claims.supporting_excerpt: After we had finished basic training at Sheppard Field, Wichita Falls, Texas... It was Army Air Corps. The Air Corps, it's now Air Force. (Segments 207-217)

claims.claim: McKinney witnessed German prisoners of war served at white-tablecloth tables while Black American soldiers ate on cardboard plates in a colored dining room at a West Texas depot.
claims.supporting_excerpt: We had cardboard plates wooden forks and somebody said, hey, come here and look. We looked from where we were in the colored dining room, in the white dining room. There were tablecloths, then in the silverware and German prisoners of war. (Segments 220-225)

claims.claim: Benjamin Elijah Mays became president of Morehouse, holding the post from 1941 to 1967.
claims.supporting_excerpt: Benjamin Elijah Mays was president of Morehouse from 1941 to 1967. (Segment 307); also Segment 137: 'the person who became president of Morehouse four years before I entered there was Dr. Benjamin Elijah Mays.'
claims.rationale: Transcript explicitly says 1941. The historical record (and the ground-truth corpus entry for Benjamin E. Mays) gives 1940. Taking transcript as primary source per the citation-audit rule, this is treated as supported as stated; the apparent one-year discrepancy is noted as a potential McKinney memory slip but does not warrant 'unsupported' status.

claims.claim: McKinney shifted from a pre-law track to the ministry under the influence of George Kelsey, who taught the required Bible course.
claims.supporting_excerpt: George Kelsey. I don't know. Sometimes people say you're preaching to me. I didn't know but he was speaking to me. In fact, a lot of fellows were thinking about being lawyers. And some of them were considering the ministry and how were they going to reconcile the two. And then he said, the law can only function after something has been done. (Segments 281-287)

claims.claim: McKinney pastored in Providence, Rhode Island, for three years and three months before Seattle.
claims.supporting_excerpt: I came here from, I passed it in Providence, Rhode Island, for three years and three months. (Segments 651-652)

claims.claim: McKinney received the call to Mount Zion Baptist Church in late November 1957 and began the first Sunday in February 1958.
claims.supporting_excerpt: Now, I came here and I received the call of that the latter part of November 1957. But I moved in and began the first Sunday in February 1958. (Segments 644-645)

claims.claim: McKinney invited Martin Luther King Jr. to speak in Seattle in 1961, and King told McKinney this was his last solo trip.
claims.supporting_excerpt: He made one trip to Seattle at an invitation. And that was the last time he traveled by himself... He said after his trip here was his last trip anywhere by himself. We invited him here... to speak of the brotherhood of the church. And he accepted it. And this was in 1961. (Segments 381-390)

claims.claim: First Presbyterian Church initially agreed to host the King event and then withdrew, leading to a confrontational meeting where the head of the church council declined the venue; the event was relocated to another downtown venue.
claims.supporting_excerpt: And the fellow who was the executive of the Presbytery of Seattle suggested that I approach Presbyterian Church, Seattle... we got closer to the time and started advertising, I got a letter from the ruling body of the church stating that you could not have the event there... We never saw these documents that you said we refused to sign or didn't sign... The doctor came, will come. He will speak. And I said, we'll have to tell the world about what you did. (Segments 394-440)

claims.claim: In 1998, McKinney received an unsolicited letter of apology from First Presbyterian Church approximately 37 or 38 years after the incident.
claims.supporting_excerpt: Just before I retired in 1998... the content of the letter was simply that 37 or 38 years ago, or even justice was done to your church and to you and relish to apologize for that. (Segments 441-450)

claims.claim: The Oregon Territory had a law making it illegal for Black people to permanently settle, not repealed until 1926.
claims.supporting_excerpt: There was a law that wasn't repeal until 1926 that he had discovered in America that made it illegal to blackstrap permanently settle in the Oregon territory. (Segments 535-537); also Segment 594: 'It wasn't repealed until 1926.'

claims.claim: George Washington Bush led a wagon train of Black settlers from Missouri who, refused permanent settlement in the Oregon Territory, crossed the Columbia and settled near present-day Olympia, Washington.
claims.supporting_excerpt: There was a man named George Washington Bush... who led a wagon train of folks west from Missouri. And when they got to, when they got to Portland, they were told they couldn't jump permanently suddenly, but they crossed the river to Fort Vancouver... up around where the state capital is, here around Lacey, Washington, Olympia. (Segments 567-613)

claims.claim: One of George Washington Bush's sons served in the last territorial legislature and the first state legislature of Washington.
claims.supporting_excerpt: But one of George Washington Bush's sons was in the last territorial legislature and in the first state legislature of the state of Washington. (Segments 632-634)

claims.claim: Mount Zion Baptist Church (Seattle) was organized in 1890.
claims.supporting_excerpt: Our church was organized in 1890, one year after the Washington became a state. (Segments 718-719)

claims.claim: The Central Area Civil Rights Committee was organized in 1963 with Rev. John Adams of First AME as a leading figure.
claims.supporting_excerpt: I know that in 1963, you were part of the group that organized what became known as the CACRC... As a Rev. John Adams, who was the pastor first in me at the time, was the most successful leader of that group. He's now a retired bishop in the AME church living in Atlanta. (Segments 814-827)
claims.rationale: The 1963 founding date and Rev. John Adams's leadership role are supported. The 'First AME' designation is inferred from the transcript's ambiguous 'pastor first in me at the time' (likely a transcription artifact for 'First AME at the time') plus the later identification of Adams as a retired AME bishop. Partially supported because the institutional name is not in clean transcript form.

claims.claim: The Frederick & Nelson department store march resulted in opening Black employment at the store.
claims.supporting_excerpt: But I think the big march on. Frederick & Nelson really opened up a lot of George R. Remember one fellow. He was a member of our congregation. He was had a critical of me and my participation. And told us we shouldn't march against Frederick & Nelson. But when we did it and was successful, he was one of the first persons to apply it for a job. (Segments 854-859)

claims.claim: Mount Zion's current sanctuary was built/replaced in 1974, after local banks refused funding; financing came in part from the American Baptist Home Mission Society and Mount Zion's own credit union.
claims.supporting_excerpt: Then we replaced in 1974... So we did have the help of the American Baptist Home Mission Society as well as people borrowing from the credit union... we didn't get money from one single local bank. (Segments 907-911, 957)

claims.claim: The Seattle Opportunities Industrialization Center at 22nd and Jackson was built on the model of Rev. Leon H. Sullivan of Philadelphia.
claims.supporting_excerpt: Now another piece, it's 22nd and Jackson. It's a Seattle Vocational Institute. But that venue was initially the Seattle OIC Opportunities Industrialization Center. That after President of that, Dr. Rev. Leon H. Sullivan of Philadelphia started that movement. (Segments 913-916)

claims.claim: Rep. Jim McDermott helped have the state take over the Seattle OIC facility after Reagan-era cuts.
claims.supporting_excerpt: But when Reagan became, he pulled a rug out from under. A lot of those community efforts set better themselves. And Jim McDermott, who's a representative from the state, stepped in and where we were on the verge of bankruptcy, he had the state to take over. (Segments 919-922)

claims.claim: McKinney helped organize Liberty Bank, which later went out of business.
claims.supporting_excerpt: I was a part of the group that helped organize the Liberty Bank, which later went out of business and the facility that it built in 24th and union. (Segments 931-932)

claims.claim: Adam Clayton Powell Jr. was invited annually by McKinney's father to Antioch Baptist Church for the church anniversary in January, from 1936 through 1946.
claims.supporting_excerpt: And he would also bring, I think from 1936 through 46. He brought for the church anniversary in January, Adam Clayton Powell Jr. (Segments 370-371)

claims.claim: Mordecai Wyatt Johnson was president of Howard University and a Morehouse graduate who served as a mentor figure to McKinney's father.
claims.supporting_excerpt: Many people of that statue of the Mordecai Wyatt Johnson who was president of Howard University at that time, and also a more house graduate. And sort of a mentor, older brother figure, to my father when he was in college. (Segments 181-183)
claims.rationale: Directly stated; corroborated by ground-truth corpus entry for Mordecai Wyatt Johnson.

publication_decision.rationale: The main summary's accuracy score is 84 and quality score is 87, both below the 90/90 publication threshold. The citation audit also flagged two partially supported claims: (1) Benjamin E. Mays's Morehouse presidency dates (transcript says 1941, ground-truth corpus says 1940 - a one-year discrepancy that almost certainly reflects a small McKinney memory slip), and (2) the institutional identification of Rev. John Adams's 'First AME' church (transcript reads 'pastor first in me at the time,' a transcription artifact, leaving the institution inferred rather than directly attested). Both partial flags route to human review per the fail-closed gate. The interview is rich, substantively significant, and historically valuable - a human reviewer should be able to clear it for publication after confirming the Mays dates against external biographical sources and the John Adams / First AME identification against Seattle AME records. No fabricated claims were detected, and no facts contradict the ground-truth corpus.
